content: TBI diagnosis uses Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS, score 3-15): Eye opening (1-4), Verbal response (1-5), Motor response (1-6). GCS 13-15 = mild, 9-12 = moderate, 3-8 = severe (intubate if ≤8). Canadian CT Head Rule/New Orleans Criteria guide imaging in mild TBI. Non-contrast CT head emergently for: GCS <15, focal deficit, vomiting, severe headache, age >65, coagulopathy, dangerous mechanism, loss of consciousness/amnesia. CT findings: skull fracture, intracranial hemorrhage (epidural, subdural, subarachnoid, intraparenchymal), cerebral edema, midline shift, mass effect. MRI more sensitive for diffuse axonal injury (microhemorrhages on SWI/GRE), small contusions. Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) research tool for white matter integrity. Biomarkers under investigation: GFAP, UCH-L1 (FDA-cleared to rule out need for CT in mild TBI). Marshall/Rotterdam CT scores quantify injury severity and predict outcome. Differential diagnosis: stroke, seizure/postictal state, intoxication, metabolic encephalopathy. Prognosis: GCS motor score most predictive; pupillary reactivity important; IMPACT/CRASH calculators estimate outcome.
